coredata.dc:title: An excellent stable fluorescent probe: Selective and sensitive detection of trace amounts of Hg+2 ions in natural source of water
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Ranjana
Rai, Sunil Kumar
Tiwari, Manish Kumar
Mishra, Anurag
Tewari, Ashish K.
Mishra, Phool Chand
Singh, Ranjan K.
coredata.dc:description: The fluorescent and stable aqueous solution of 3-Cyano-4,6-dimethyl-2-pyridone (CDPy) has been used as a probe for selective detection of Hg+2 ions in natural river water. The fluorescence intensity of the probe was reduced on adding Hg+2 ions. The DFT and TD-DFT confirmed that the reduction in fluorescence intensity occurred due to excited state charge transfer between the CDPy molecule and Hg+2 ions. This procedure can be used to detect Hg+2 ions up to a detection limit ∼0.41μM. It is reproducible with excellent stability (∼10weeks) and applicable in a wide linear range (3–231μM).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent probe
3-Cyano-4,6-dimethyl-2-pyridone
Natural river water
Hg+2 ions
Stability
LOD

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV–VIS, emission spectra and SEM of CDPy
Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the probe
Detection of Hg+2 ions in the natural river water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Visible absorption (black color), photoluminescence excitation (blue color) and emission spectra (red color) of CDPy (0.20mM) in the river water river water λex =323nm and λem =374nm. (b) The international commission on illumination diagram (CIE) for fluorescent probe. (c) 2D matrices of the PL spectra of probe under different excitation wavelength varying from 323nm to 350nm. (d) Excitation dependent photoluminescence spectra of the probe (inset shows the optical image of the probe irradiated by UV lamp of wavelength 365nm (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

(a) PL intensity of the fluorescent probe and its complex with various metal ions each having concentration 90μM and λex =323nm. (b) Normalized PL intensity of the fluorescent probe along with that of its complex of various metal ions shown by bar graph. (c) PL intensity of the five fluorescent probes prepared in similar conditions. (d) Variation of PL response of a fluorescent probe with respect to time.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of fluorescent probe CDPy (0.20mM, prepared in the river water) upon addition of various concentration of Hg+2 (from top second to bottom: 3μM, 42μM, 81μM, 118μM, 153μM, 186μM, 216μM, 231μM) excited at 323nm. (b) The linear dependence of F0/F on the concentration of Hg+2 within the range of (3μM-231μM).

(a) Optimized geometry of CDPy. (b) Optimized geometry and NBO charges at 8O, 11N and 20Hg+2 of CDPy+Hg+2 system in aqueous media employing IEF-PCM solvation model, obtained at B3LYP/LanL2DZ level of density functional theory (DFT) in the ground state. (c) Optimized geometry of CDPy. (d) Optimized geometry and NBO charges at 8O, 11N and 20Hg+2 of CDPy+Hg+2 system in aqueous media employing IEF-PCM solvation model, obtained at B3LYP/LanL2DZ level of density functional theory (TD-DFT) in the excited state.
Comparison of sensing parameters of different fluorescent probes for Hg+2 detection.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Molecule containing fluorophore quinoline group and a water-soluble d-glucosamine group.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: TSRh6g-β-cyclodextrin fluorophore/adamantine-modified inclusion complex magnetic nanoparticles.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: NBO charge distributions in CDPy and CDPy+Hg+2 complexes in the ground and lowest singlet excited state obtained at B3LYP/LanL2DZ level of density functional theory in aqueous media.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The optimized structure (ground and excited states) of CDPy and CDPy+Hg+2 in aqueous medium have been shown in Fig. 4(a)–(d).

head.ce:title: An excellent stable fluorescent probe: Selective and sensitive detection of trace amounts of Hg+2 ions in natural source of water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Dravyaguna, Institute of Medical Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorescent and stable aqueous solution of 3-Cyano-4,6-dimethyl-2-pyridone (CDPy) has been used as a probe for selective detection of Hg+2 ions in natural river water. The fluorescence intensity of the probe was reduced on adding Hg+2 ions. The DFT and TD-DFT confirmed that the reduction in fluorescence intensity occurred due to excited state charge transfer between the CDPy molecule and Hg+2 ions. This procedure can be used to detect Hg+2 ions up to a detection limit ∼0.41μM. It is reproducible with excellent stability (∼10weeks) and applicable in a wide linear range (3–231μM).

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ion pollution has become a critical issue worldwide due to its severe risk on human health and environment. Mercury ion is a well recognized bio-accumulative and highly toxic pollutant. Its presence even at very low concentrations might result in serious health problems [1–3]. Its continuous exposure may cause serious adverse effects on central human nervous systems, kidney failure, various cognitive and movement disorders [3–6]. Hg+2 ions are widely distributed in the environment through a number of chemical and biological pathways. With the passage of time, significant amount of mercury have accumulated in the biosphere due to anthropogenic and non-anthropogenic sources. The major source of human exposure to Hg+2 ions is contaminated natural sources of water [2,4]. The inorganic form of Hg+2 is highly toxic due its good water solubility and stability. Therefore cost effective, rapid, user friendly, sensitive, stable and selective detection of Hg+2 is an important task so that trace amount of Hg+2 in the natural sources of water could be measured.

Many traditional methods have been reported to determine the trace amounts of heavy metals. These methods include atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy [7], total reflection X-ray fluorescence spectrometry [8], anodic stripping voltammetry [9], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [10] and selective cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry [11]. All these methods can be utilized for sensitive and selective detection of Hg+2 ions. However, these methods require complicated sample preparation which imposes limitations on their use. Fluorescence and electrochemical properties of some metal based nano-materials (Au and Ag nano-particles) have also been reported for the detection of Hg+2 [12,13]. These techniques also require multiple steps and extensive pretreatment of the electrode surface [14]. Gold nano-particle based Hyper Rayleigh Scattering (HRS) assay can also be used to determine different heavy metal ions but due to lack of selectivity this technique cannot be applied to detect the heavy metal ions in real samples [15]. The semiconductor quantum dots exhibiting high emission quantum yield and size tunable emission profile have become most extensive optical sensors [16]. But the toxicity associated with the semiconductor quantum dots limits their applications [17]. Significant progress has been achieved with the development of carbon quantum dot [3,18–20], nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon quantum dot [21–25] based sensors to detect the trace amounts of heavy metal ions in living cells and natural sources of water. However, there are some limitations associated with these quantum dots such as lack of reliable mechanism for photoluminescence as their structures are not known precisely till now. Hence it is difficult to determine the exact mechanism of their fluorescing behavior if used for detection of Hg+2 ions.

The present work is focused on the development of a selective, sensitive, stable, rapid and simple analytical method for the detection of trace amounts of mercury in the natural sources of water. 2-Pyridone derivatives have a wide range of biological activity [26–28] as well as coordination property with transition metals as 1,3-bridging ligands [29]. 3-Cyano-4,6-dimethyl-2-pyridone (CDPy) has been synthesized and characterized by known methods. In our study 3-cyano-4,6-dimethyl-2-pyridone (CDPy) has been used as a potential ligand for the sensing of Hg+2. The reason for choosing this derivative is; (1) Pyridone is less flexible and could form the tetra-nucleating (this bonding mode has not been observed) and tri-nucleating pyridones [29]. (2) The CDPy possesses an additional coordination group (CN), therefore, possibility of tetra-nucleation increases; (3) the coordination chemistry of 6-methyl and 3-nitrile derivatives are well known [30]. Its photoluminescence properties have been utilized to detect Hg+2 in the natural sources of water directly.

Cyanoacetamide, Ethanol, K2CO3, Acetyl Acetone, AgNO3, KCl, HgCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, BaCl2, and CaCl2 procured from Sigma Aldrich India, were used without further purification. Natural water was collected from the nearby river Ganga, in which Hg+2 detection was done using this technique. It was kept at room temperature and used without any pretreatment. This untreated river water was used to prepare the solutions of the fluorescent probe of CDPy and all other salts.

In a 250mL round bottomed flask, cyanoacetamide [4.20g, 0.05mol] was dissolved in ethanol [50mL] followed by addition of aqueous K2CO3 [6.90g, 0.05mol]. The reaction mixture was stirred for 10min at room temperature and to that solution acetyl acetone [5.10mL, 0.05mol] was added. This reaction mixture was stirred for 20–25h at room temperature. The resulting precipitate was filtered, washed with cold ethanol and dried in air. Its properties were found to be mp. 287–289°C; Yield: 7.30g [98.63%]; 1H-NMR [500MHz, DMSO- d
6, 25°C, TMS] δH 2.13 [s, 3H, CH3], 2.25 [s, 3H, CH3], 6.09 [s, 1H, ArH], 13C-NMR [125MHz, DMSO- d
6, 25°C, TMS] δc 20.3, 21.0, 98.4, 107.6, 117.4, 153.2, 159.5, 163.1; IR [KBr, cm−1]: 1660 [CO stretching], 2219 [CN stretching], 3138 [NH stretching]; Elemental analysis; calculated; C, 64.86%; H, 5.40%; N, 18.91%, observed; C, 64.85%; H, 5.41%; N, 18.90%.
The mechanism of CDPy synthesis, 1H-NMR and 13C-NMR, FTIR and Raman spectra have been provided in the supplementary material represented by Figs. S1, S2, S3 and S4, respectively.

Morphological characterization of synthesized CDPy was carried out using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Philips XL 20) while, spectroscopic characterizations were performed using different techniques; FTIR (Spectrum 65 FT-IR spectrometer, Perkin Elmer), Raman (In-via Raman spectrometer, Renishaw), Nuclear magnetic resonance (JEOL AL 500MHz FT-NMR Spectrometer) and UV–Visible absorption (Lambda 25 UV–Visible spectrometer, Perkin Elmer) spectroscopy. The emission and excitation spectra were recorded using an Edinburg FLS 900 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Photonics Division, Edinburg Instruments) using a Xenon lamp for source of excitation. The spectral slit width in this study was kept fixed ∼1nm for both the excitation and the emission monochromator channels during all the fluorescence measurements.

The quantum yield (Q) of CDPy was obtained by measuring fluorescence intensity based on the following equation,
(1)
Q
S
=
Q
R
·
I
S
·
A
R
I
R
·
A
S
where Q, I, and A are quantum yield, measured fluorescence intensity and optical density at the excitation wavelength, respectively. Here, the subscript ‘R’ refers to the standard with known quantum yield and ‘S’ for the sample. The quantum yield was measured using quinine sulphate in 0.1M sulfuric acid solution (quantum yield=0.54 at 310nm) as the standard. In order to minimize re-absorption effects, absorbance was kept below 0.05 [22].

The Geometry optimization and vibrational frequency calculations for CDPy were performed in the aqueous medium using the Becke’s [31] non-local three parameters exchange correlation functional along with the Lee-Yang-Parr correlation functional (B3LYP) [32] with the LanL2DZ basis set. Geometry optimization and vibrational frequency calculations of CDPy+Hg+2 systems were also performed in aqueous medium at the same level of theory. All these theoretical calculations were performed using the Gaussian-09 [33] program package. The Gauss-View version 5.0 [34] was used to visualize the optimized structures and their vibrational modes. Natural bond orbital (NBO) [35,36] charges were calculated on each atom of CDPy and CDPy+Hg+2 in aqueous medium. The TD-DFT method was used to calculate electronic transitions. The bulk solvent effect of water on these systems was treated by using the polarisable continuum model (PCM) [37,38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–VIS, emission spectra and SEM of CDPy
ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous [∼0.20mM] solutions of CDPy were prepared in triple distilled water as well as in river water. Its UV–Visible absorption and fluorescence spectra of both the solutions were recorded by taking corresponding references. It was found that the absorption and emission spectra of both solutions are identical. Therefore, to prepare fluorescent probe, CDPy was dissolved in real sample [river water] directly and were used for selective detection of Hg+2 ions. These data (UV–Visible absorption and emission spectra of CDPy prepared in triple distilled and river water) have been provided in the supplementary material (Figs. S5 and S6). The stock solution of CDPy prepared in the river water was used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Hg+2 ions in the real river water. The absorption spectrum of CDPy in the river water shown in Fig.1 (a) has three absorption peaks at 323, 237 and 212nm. These peaks are attributed to π(Py/CN)→π∗(Py/CN), π(Py/CN)→π∗(Py/CO) and π(Py/CN)/n(CH3)→π∗(Py/CN) transitions, respectively.

The fluorescence spectrum of the sample when excited by 323nm wavelength shows strong emission peak centered at wavelength 374nm having stokes shift ∼51nm and quantum yield ∼0.50. It is clear that CDPy emits in the blue region of the electromagnetic spectrum. The color of the emitted light was calculated by using International Commission on Illumination (CIE) software which was also found in the blue region shown in Fig.1(b). Emission spectra were also recorded at three different excitation wavelengths; 323, 340 and 350nm and the emission peak were observed at 374nm. It is excitation independent as suggested by Kasha’s rule as shown in Fig.1(d). The 323nm excitation wavelength gives maximum emission. This process has also been represented by the contour plot in Fig.1(c). The fluorescent probe appeared blue in UV-Lamp of wavelength 365nm. It has been shown in the inset of Fig.1(d). Morphology of as synthesized CDPy was obtained through scanning electron microscopic (SEM) characterization. This shows rod like texture of CDPy represented in Fig. S7 (supplementary material).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the probe
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity of the fluorescent probe was evaluated by PL responses with 323nm excitation after adding 90μM aqueous solutions of various metal ions; Ag+, Hg++, Mg++, Ca++, K+, and Ba++ (see Fig.2 (a)). These aqueous solutions were also prepared in river water directly. In comparison to the other metal ions significant PL reduction was observed with the addition of 90μM Hg+2 as shown in Fig.2(b). It is clear that the prepared fluorescent probe is very specific for Hg+2. Reproducibility was checked by the PL response of the five probes of the same concentration prepared in river water excited at the same wavelength 323nm shown in Fig.2(c). The changes in the PL intensities of the different probes were observed much less than 0.5%. From Fig.2(c), it is obvious that this fluorescent probe exhibits good reproducibility. To evaluate the stability of these sensing system PL responses were measured at the regular intervals of one week. It was seen that at the end of ten weeks the PL intensity was found to be decrease less than 2% to that of the initial one. Hence this fluorescent probe was found to be stable up to the end of ten weeks (Fig.2(d)). The PL intensity was measured three times for each fluorescent probe and its average values were calculated. After that, average error was calculated for PL intensity measurement of the fluorescent probe which was found to be less than ∼2% as represented by error bars in Fig.2(d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg+2 ions in the natural river water
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of the bare probe and after adding Hg+2 at various concentrations [3–231μM] have been shown in Fig.3 (a). The peak position of emission remains unchanged at 374nm but a gradual decrease in fluorescence peak intensity was observed on increasing Hg+2 different concentrations. Measurement of fluorescence was performed in each case three times and average of the peak intensity was calculated. Average error was also calculated which was found below ∼1%. Quenching efficiency was calculated by F0/F, where F0 and F are fluorescence intensities excited at 323nm in the absence and presence of Hg+2, respectively. The quenching efficiency (F0/F) versus concentrations of Hg+2 shows a good linear relationship Fig.3(b). This can be represented by linear equation with intercept 0.954±0.011 and slope 0.110±0.002. Therefore, fluorescent probe can be used in a wide linear range of Hg+2 concentration varying from 3μM to 231μM in comparison to the previously reported fluorescent probes represented in Table 1 . The values of linear correlation coefficient [R2] and standard deviation [SD] were obtained as 0.998 and 0.0152, respectively by linear fitting of the graph as depicted in (Fig.3(b)). The wide linear range response of the fluorescent probe with the addition of Hg+2 ions clearly indicates the presence of dynamic quenching. It is clear that quenching was observed due to charge transfer among the molecules used to prepare fluorescent probe and Hg+2 ions in the excited state. The mechanism of this process has been strongly supported by theoretical calculations. The limit of detection [LOD] was estimated to be 0.41μM. It has been calculated based on three times the standard deviation [SD] rule [LOD=3∗SD/m] where m is the slope of the linearly fitted graph [22].

The Ground state optimized geometries of CDPy and CDPy+Hg+2 systems in aqueous media employing the IEF-PCM solvation model [37,38] were obtained at the B3LYP/LanL2DZ level of density functional theory (DFT) have been shown in Fig.4 (a) and (b), respectively. Absence of any imaginary frequency for the ground state optimized geometries ensures the minimum energy on the potential energy surface. Excited state optimized geometries of CDPy and CDPy+Hg+2 systems in aqueous media were also obtained by including the IEF-PCM solvation model with B3LYP/LanL2DZ level using the time dependent density functional (TD-DFT) theory. However, vibrational frequency analyses were not done for excited states. Excited state optimized geometries of CDPy and CDPy+Hg+2 have been shown in Fig.4(c) and (d), respectively. To confirm the charge transfer taking place in experimental fluorescence measurement, the natural bonding orbital (NBO) charges were also calculated at each atomic site of CDPy and CDPy+Hg+2 systems in aqueous media at B3LYP/LanL2DZ level of density functional theory in both ground state and excited state. The NBO charge on each atom of these systems has been presented in Table 2 . It is clear from Table 2 that charges on the 8O, 11N and Hg+2 ion in the ground state were −0.787, −0.404 and 1.945 |e|, respectively whereas corresponding charges were found to be equal −0.206, −0.019 and 0.987 |e| in the excited state. From these theoretical NBO charges calculations, it is obvious that the charge transfer takes place from 8O and 11N to Hg+2 ion in going from the ground to the excited state which is responsible for dynamic fluorescence quenching of the fluorescent probe with the addition of various concentration of Hg+2 ions.

In this work a highly fluorescent probe was prepared by dissolving CDPy directly in river water. This probe was found to be highly efficient and sensitive for a selective detection of Hg+2 ions exhibiting good reproducibility and excellent stability. This probe was used to detect presence of Hg+2 ions in wide range i.e. 3–231μM with limit of detection 0.41μM and fluorescence intensity was found to be proportional to Hg+2 ion concentration. The fluorescent probe appears to be superior to other probes to detect Hg+2 in view of excellent performance parameters.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Ranjana Singh is grateful to UGC, New Delhi, Govt. of India for providing financial support in terms of BHU research fellowship. SKR acknowledges to CSIR New-Delhi for SRF. Ranjan K. Singh is grateful to AvH Foundation, Germany, DST and CSIR, New Delhi, Govt. of India for support to procure Micro-Raman setup.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2017.03.046.
